name: Glass Making
tagline: Melting silica with fluxes into an amorphous solid — transparent ceramic for vessels, lenses, and fibres
problem:
Clay becomes opaque pottery; humans wanted translucency — to see liquids inside containers, to transmit light for windows, and eventually to shape lenses that extend vision. Silica alone melts at a temperature difficult for early furnaces; the glass problem is fluxing: adding soda, potash, or lime to break up the silica network, lowering viscosity and working range.

Glass also demands thermal patience: rapid cooling shatters due to internal stresses. Annealing — holding near the glass transition then cooling slowly — converts a museum of internal stresses into a durable object.

Chemically, glass is a frozen liquid: disordered network. That makes it corrosive-resistant compared with metals in many contexts, but brittle — shock and scratch concentration limit structural roles until tempered and laminated technologies appear millennia later.
overview:
Soda-lime glass, the bulk of modern windows and bottles, combines silica sand, sodium carbonate (historically from natron or ashes), and calcium oxide (from limestone) to stabilise against water attack. In a furnace at roughly 1000–1200 °C batch reactions form melt; working occurs at lower temperatures on the glory hole or punty iron.

Colour comes from transition metal oxides: iron greens, cobalt blues, manganese purples. Decolourisers and oxidising/reducing flames interact subtly — glass chemistry is visibly readable to the trained eye.

Forming methods evolve from core-formed vessels to blown glass — a inflation process that uses surface tension to create thin, even walls. Flat glass historically was cast or crown-blown; modern float glass rides on molten tin — outside primitive scope but noted as descendant.

Recycling cullet lowers melt energy — an ancient and modern efficiency lever.

principles.name: Network formers and modifiers
principles.explanation: Silicon dioxide forms the continuous random network; alkali oxides insert non-bridging oxygens, lowering viscosity; lime acts as a stabiliser reducing solubility in water.

principles.name: Viscosity curves
principles.explanation: Glass is not a single melting point — it softens over a range; working, annealing, and strain points are milestones along the viscosity-temperature curve.

principles.name: Thermal stress
principles.explanation: Temperature gradients during cooling create differential contraction; annealing allows stress relaxation before locking in below transition.

principles.name: Devitrification
principles.explanation: Held too long in crystallisation-prone compositions, glass can nucleate crystals — undesirable in most vessel glass.

components.name: Glass furnace / glory hole
components.function: Provides continuous high temperature for melting and reheating
components.criticalNote: Eye protection against infrared — even without visible flame

components.name: Crucible or pot melt
components.criticalNote: Metal inclusions from tools dissolve and tint glass

components.name: Blowpipe / punty
components.function: Transfers molten glass and enables inflation
components.madeFrom: Iron or stainless steel hollow rod

components.name: Marver plate
components.function: Rolls and shapes gather on a cool steel plate
components.criticalNote: Uneven marver cooling sets up asymmetric stress

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Glass Making is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Silica sand
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Lunar regolith high in SiO2 after beneficiation
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Wash to remove organics; screen grain size

rawMaterials.name: Soda ash (natron or ash)
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Leach, boil, crystallise if making ash soda

rawMaterials.name: Limestone
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Calcine to quicklime if needed before batching

buildSteps.title: Batch and mix
buildSteps.description: Weigh silica, soda, and lime to target composition. Dry mix thoroughly. Small colour tests in crucibles before large melts.

buildSteps.title: Initial melt
buildSteps.description: Ramp furnace slowly to drive off carbonates as CO2 — foaming stage needs headspace. Hold until bubbles refine.
buildSteps.warningNote: Hot glass looks like cold glass — assume everything in the shop is hot

buildSteps.title: Gather and marver
buildSteps.description: Coat blowpipe with glass paste on first dip, reheat, gather uniform mass. Roll on marver to cone symmetry.
buildSteps.warningNote: Drips cause foot burns — leather shoes insufficient

buildSteps.title: Blow and tool
buildSteps.description: Inflate with steady breath, reheat often, use jacks and blocks to shape. Transfer to punty for opening vessel mouth.
buildSteps.warningNote: Implosion risk if wall too thin — wear safety glasses

buildSteps.title: Anneal
buildSteps.description: Place in kiln at annealing soak temperature for glass type; ramp down over hours to days by thickness.
buildSteps.warningNote: Skipping anneal guarantees delayed spontaneous breakage

history.event: Mesopotamian and Egyptian glass core-forming traditions produce small vessels and beads.
Glassblowing emerges in the Roman Levant, revolutionising vessel production speed and form diversity.

impact:
Glass changed architecture (windows), science (lenses and laboratory ware), and communication (fibre optics centuries later). It is fragile but information-transparent — a different design axis than metal or ceramic.

Off-world, transparent habitats and solar concentrators make glass chemistry strategically relevant once silica sources are local.
